verses.text: Jacob settles in the land of his fathers' sojourning — Canaan. Joseph at seventeen is shepherding with his brothers, and he brings their father a bad report. The Hebrew word evil here can mean bad as in shameful conduct. He is the youngest watching the older boys.

Jacob settles in the land of his fathers' sojourning — Canaan. Joseph at seventeen is shepherding with his brothers, and he brings their father a bad report. The Hebrew word evil here can mean bad as in shameful conduct. He is the youngest watching the older boys.

Israel loved Joseph more than all his other sons — the son of his old age, born to Rachel. He gave him a tunic of many colours. His brothers saw their father's favor and hated him. They could not speak a peaceable word to him.

Israel loved Joseph more than all his other sons — the son of his old age, born to Rachel. He gave him a tunic of many colours. His brothers saw their father's favor and hated him. They could not speak a peaceable word to him.

Joseph dreams: we were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf rose and stood upright while yours gathered around and bowed to mine. He tells the dream to his brothers. They hate him even more for telling it.

Joseph dreams: we were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf rose and stood upright while yours gathered around and bowed to mine. He tells the dream to his brothers. They hate him even more for telling it.

Joseph dreams: we were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf rose and stood upright while yours gathered around and bowed to mine. He tells the dream to his brothers. They hate him even more for telling it.

Shalt thou indeed reign over us? they ask. Will you actually have dominion over us? The dream's content is exactly what they have feared most. They hated him still more for his dreams, and for his words.

A second dream: the sun, the moon, and eleven stars bow to me. This time he tells his father too. Israel rebukes him: what is this dream? Shall I, your mother, and your brothers really come and bow to you? Even the favorite parent finds this hard.

A second dream: the sun, the moon, and eleven stars bow to me. This time he tells his father too. Israel rebukes him: what is this dream? Shall I, your mother, and your brothers really come and bow to you? Even the favorite parent finds this hard.
His brothers envy him. But his father observes the saying — he files it away. Some hard words need to be held in reserve, not answered yet. The patriarch has learned to wait.

The brothers go to feed the flock near Shechem — the very place that bled the city in chapter 34. Israel sends Joseph: go see how your brothers are. Here am I, Joseph answers. He doesn't know how dangerous his obedience is about to become.

The brothers go to feed the flock near Shechem — the very place that bled the city in chapter 34. Israel sends Joseph: go see how your brothers are. Here am I, Joseph answers. He doesn't know how dangerous his obedience is about to become.

The brothers go to feed the flock near Shechem — the very place that bled the city in chapter 34. Israel sends Joseph: go see how your brothers are. Here am I, Joseph answers. He doesn't know how dangerous his obedience is about to become.
A man finds Joseph wandering in the field: what are you looking for? My brothers. The man tells him they have moved to Dothan. Joseph follows. Even a stranger in the road becomes part of the plan.
A man finds Joseph wandering in the field: what are you looking for? My brothers. The man tells him they have moved to Dothan. Joseph follows. Even a stranger in the road becomes part of the plan.
A man finds Joseph wandering in the field: what are you looking for? My brothers. The man tells him they have moved to Dothan. Joseph follows. Even a stranger in the road becomes part of the plan.

They see him from a distance and conspire before he arrives: here comes that dreamer. Let us kill him, throw him in a pit, say a wild animal ate him — and then we shall see what becomes of his dreams. They mock the dreams they secretly fear.

They see him from a distance and conspire before he arrives: here comes that dreamer. Let us kill him, throw him in a pit, say a wild animal ate him — and then we shall see what becomes of his dreams. They mock the dreams they secretly fear.

They see him from a distance and conspire before he arrives: here comes that dreamer. Let us kill him, throw him in a pit, say a wild animal ate him — and then we shall see what becomes of his dreams. They mock the dreams they secretly fear.

Reuben — the oldest, the one who had failed his father with Bilhah — tries to save him. Don't kill him. Throw him in this pit, but don't lay a hand on him. He plans to come back later and deliver Joseph to their father.

Reuben — the oldest, the one who had failed his father with Bilhah — tries to save him. Don't kill him. Throw him in this pit, but don't lay a hand on him. He plans to come back later and deliver Joseph to their father.
When Joseph arrives, they strip him of his coat and throw him into a pit. The pit is empty — no water. The brothers have removed the symbol of his father's love. And then they sit down to eat.
When Joseph arrives, they strip him of his coat and throw him into a pit. The pit is empty — no water. The brothers have removed the symbol of his father's love. And then they sit down to eat.

Then they look up and see a caravan of Ishmaelite merchants coming from Gilead, their camels loaded with spices and balm and myrrh, headed down to Egypt. A way to be rid of Joseph without his blood on their hands.

Judah speaks: what gain is there if we kill our brother and cover up his blood? Let us sell him to the Ishmaelites. Let not our hand be upon him — for he is our brother, our flesh. His brothers agreed. The bargain is settled in moments.

Judah speaks: what gain is there if we kill our brother and cover up his blood? Let us sell him to the Ishmaelites. Let not our hand be upon him — for he is our brother, our flesh. His brothers agreed. The bargain is settled in moments.

Midianite merchants pass by; the brothers pull Joseph out of the pit and sell him for twenty pieces of silver. The merchants take him down to Egypt. The exact route God will later use for an exodus begins with a brother being sold.

Reuben — who had not been there for the sale — returns to the pit and finds it empty. He tears his clothes and goes to the others: the boy is not there. And I, where shall I go? The oldest brother's plan has failed quietly.

Reuben — who had not been there for the sale — returns to the pit and finds it empty. He tears his clothes and goes to the others: the boy is not there. And I, where shall I go? The oldest brother's plan has failed quietly.

They take Joseph's coat, kill a young goat, dip the coat in its blood. They send it to their father: we have found this. Is it your son's coat? Jacob knows it instantly: an evil beast has devoured him. Joseph is surely torn to pieces. The lie lands exactly where they aimed it.

They take Joseph's coat, kill a young goat, dip the coat in its blood. They send it to their father: we have found this. Is it your son's coat? Jacob knows it instantly: an evil beast has devoured him. Joseph is surely torn to pieces. The lie lands exactly where they aimed it.

They take Joseph's coat, kill a young goat, dip the coat in its blood. They send it to their father: we have found this. Is it your son's coat? Jacob knows it instantly: an evil beast has devoured him. Joseph is surely torn to pieces. The lie lands exactly where they aimed it.

Jacob tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and mourns many days. All his sons and daughters come to comfort him, but he refuses comfort: I will go down to the grave unto my son mourning. His father wept for him. Grief sometimes has no shortcut.

Jacob tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and mourns many days. All his sons and daughters come to comfort him, but he refuses comfort: I will go down to the grave unto my son mourning. His father wept for him. Grief sometimes has no shortcut.

Meanwhile, the Midianites sold Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, captain of Pharaoh's guard. The boy his father is weeping for is alive — and in the household of a powerful man. The chapter ends with the brothers' deception still standing, and God's plan already moving.
